meta.scoring_logic: Knowledge_score = mean(q3, q8, q13, q18) / 3 * 100 + 40% open2 blend (10% weight)

audit.summary: 15 of 20 current Knowledge questions suffer from STRUCTURAL FLAW — they measure learning channel preference or knowledge management preference, not epistemic discipline under uncertainty. Only 5 have minor contamination with some redeemable elements. Zero questions fully satisfy the Cognitive Tension Rule.

items.current_stem: How do you prefer to learn new skills?
problems: Learning modality preference, not epistemic discipline
No cognitive tension (all options equally benign)
Options lean toward 'more research = better' hierarchy

items.current_stem: How do you organize and share acquired knowledge?
problems: Knowledge management inventory, not epistemic discipline
No uncertainty or constraint in scenario
All options are equally valid 'best practices'

items.current_stem: How do you stay updated in your areas of expertise?
How do you keep your market knowledge current?
Your approach to learning a new business model:
problems: 'Build a financial model' cross-loads with Computational Power
Some tension between approaches but weak

items.current_stem: Institutional knowledge is best maintained by:
problems: 'Best maintained' implies there IS a best answer

items.current_stem: To onboard into a new industry fast, you:
problems: Mild CP cross-loading on 'Map the value chain'

items.current_stem: How do you stay current in your craft?
Building domain knowledge for a client, you:
problems: But options are input sources, not epistemic strategies

items.current_stem: To learn a new domain quickly, you:

problems: Measures pedagogical approach, not Knowledge pillar

items.current_stem: To learn a new regulation, you:

q3.question: Your team's technical decision is based on a well-cited paper, but you notice a methodology limitation that nobody else flagged. The deadline is tomorrow. You:
options: Flag the limitation and propose a quick sensitivity check on the key assumption
Accept the paper's conclusions but document the limitation for future reference
Cross-reference with an independent source that used a different methodology
Consult a colleague with domain expertise to assess whether the limitation matters in your context
q3.tension_pair: depth vs speed / independent verification vs delegation

q8.question: Two trusted data sources give contradictory readings on a system's health. Both have been reliable historically. You:
options: Investigate the measurement conditions that could explain the divergence
Weight each source by its track record in similar situations
Run an independent diagnostic to generate a third reference point
Consult the team that maintains each source to understand recent calibration changes
q8.tension_pair: certainty vs speed / evidence vs context

q13.question: You've built expertise in a niche area over months. A new team member needs to get productive quickly, but the knowledge is complex and context-dependent. You:
options: Create a structured decision tree that captures the key judgment calls
Pair with them on real cases, letting them make decisions while you observe
Write down the principles and let them discover the edge cases themselves
Point them to the source materials you used and schedule check-ins to discuss their interpretation
q13.tension_pair: structure vs adaptability / depth vs speed

q18.question: A vendor presents a new technology with impressive benchmarks, but the evaluation conditions don't match your production environment. You:
options: Design a controlled test that mirrors your actual constraints
Seek out independent users who deployed it in similar conditions
Analyze the gap between their test conditions and yours to estimate real-world performance
Request access to their raw data and re-run the analysis with your parameters
q18.tension_pair: evidence vs speed / independent verification vs structured synthesis

q3.question: Your strategy relies on a market assumption supported by one reputable analyst report. A competitor's move suggests the assumption may be wrong. You:
options: Commission a quick independent analysis to test the assumption from a different angle
Adjust the strategy to work under both the original and alternative assumptions
Talk to frontline salespeople to get qualitative signals on which assumption holds
Document the uncertainty and set a trigger point where you'd revisit the assumption
q3.tension_pair: certainty vs speed / evidence vs adaptability

q8.question: You're entering a market where the available data is sparse and contradictory. Industry experts disagree on fundamentals. You:
options: Run a small-scale pilot to generate your own data before committing
Map the disagreements to identify what specific evidence would resolve them
Adopt the view of the expert whose reasoning is most transparent, while monitoring for disconfirming signals
Build separate business cases for the two most plausible scenarios
q8.tension_pair: evidence vs speed / certainty vs openness

q13.question: Your organization has accumulated years of informal knowledge in key people's heads. A restructuring puts that knowledge at risk. You:
options: Identify the highest-risk knowledge and have holders articulate their decision frameworks
Create cross-functional pairs so knowledge transfers through working on real problems together
Document processes first and let tacit judgment develop through supervised practice
Build a lightweight knowledge audit to discover what you don't know you're about to lose
q13.tension_pair: structure vs adaptability / depth vs speed

q18.question: A regulatory change will affect your business, but the interpretation is ambiguous and industry peers are reading it differently. You:
options: Engage a legal specialist and stress-test multiple interpretations against your operations
Wait for an industry body to issue guidance, while preparing contingency plans
Adopt the most conservative interpretation now and relax it as clarity emerges
Compare how similar ambiguities were resolved historically to predict the likely outcome
q18.tension_pair: certainty vs speed / risk vs validation

q3.question: You've developed a strong visual style, but a new project requires a visual language you're unfamiliar with. The brief is vague and references are scarce. You:
options: Study the closest existing examples and identify the underlying design principles
Create quick experiments in the new style and gather feedback before committing
Interview the stakeholders to clarify what they associate with the desired visual language
Adapt elements from your existing style that could bridge into the new direction
q3.tension_pair: depth vs speed / evidence vs intuition

q8.question: Your creative direction is based on audience research, but early user testing returns mixed signals — some love it, some find it confusing. You:
options: Segment the responses to understand if different audience groups react differently
Test a simplified version to isolate whether the confusion is about concept or execution
Revisit the original research to check if you missed nuances in the data
Bring in a fresh set of testers with a clearer testing protocol to reduce noise
q8.tension_pair: evidence vs intuition / depth vs speed

q13.question: A junior designer asks you how you make judgment calls about visual hierarchy — a skill you've built intuitively over years. You:
options: Walk them through a recent project, explaining the reasoning behind each decision
Give them a set of principles and let them critique existing work to build their own eye
Have them make the calls on a low-stakes project while you review and explain the gaps
Show them contrasting examples (good vs poor hierarchy) and ask them to articulate the difference
q13.tension_pair: structure vs adaptability / independent verification vs collaborative validation

q18.question: A client insists their audience wants a specific aesthetic, but your research suggests otherwise. The client's conviction is strong. You:
options: Propose an A/B test with both approaches on a small sample before full production
Present your research transparently and ask the client to explain what evidence supports their view
Find a creative solution that incorporates elements of both perspectives
Document both positions and agree on success criteria that will resolve the disagreement after launch
q18.tension_pair: evidence vs authority / certainty vs openness

q3.question: Your team follows a standard procedure, but an unusual case doesn't fit the existing protocol. The procedure manual doesn't cover this scenario. You:
options: Identify which parts of the standard procedure still apply and adapt the rest with documented reasoning
Consult a colleague who's handled similar edge cases to understand how they approached it
Escalate to a supervisor with a clear description of why the standard procedure doesn't fit
Handle it using your best judgment and write up the case so the procedure can be updated
q3.tension_pair: structure vs adaptability / agency vs alignment

q8.question: Two experienced team members give you contradictory advice on handling a service situation. Both have strong track records. You:
options: Ask each to explain the reasoning behind their recommendation, not just the conclusion
Look at what's different about this situation compared to when each approach worked before
Try the approach with the lower downside risk and evaluate the outcome
Check if there's a documented precedent for this type of situation before deciding
q8.tension_pair: certainty vs speed / evidence vs delegation

q13.question: After a process change, frontline staff report that the new procedure works for most cases but fails on edge cases that used to be handled by informal know-how. You:
options: Collect the edge cases and identify patterns to create supplementary guidelines
Pair experienced staff with newer staff during complex cases to transfer the informal knowledge
Revise the procedure to include decision branches for the most common exceptions
Set up a quick feedback loop where staff flag edge cases as they occur so you can address them systematically
q13.tension_pair: structure vs adaptability / depth vs speed

q18.question: A new industry guideline contradicts a practice your team has used successfully for years. The guideline's evidence base is limited but growing. You:
options: Review the evidence behind the guideline and compare it with your team's outcome data
Pilot the new guideline on a subset of cases while maintaining the old practice as control
Adopt the guideline but track outcomes closely to verify it performs as claimed
Consult peers at other organizations to understand how they're interpreting and implementing it
q18.tension_pair: evidence vs authority / certainty vs openness

archetypes.name: Structured Analyst
archetypes.description: Prefers codification, documentation, and framework-based approaches

archetypes.name: Pattern Seeker
archetypes.description: Looks for analogies, historical patterns, and contextual similarities

archetypes.name: Tool Optimizer
archetypes.description: Favors experimental verification and empirical testing

archetypes.name: Social Synthesizer
archetypes.description: Relies on consultation, collaboration, and stakeholder input

archetypes.name: Knowledge Accumulator
archetypes.description: Prioritizes depth, thoroughness, and comprehensive understanding

archetypes.name: Creative Reframer
archetypes.description: Approaches knowledge through reinterpretation and novel angles

archetypes.name: Operational Executor
archetypes.description: Values speed and pragmatic decision-making over epistemic rigor

archetypes.name: Random Baseline
archetypes.description: Uniform random selection across all options

option_frequency_distribution.note: No option exceeds 29% predicted selection across any question. All within acceptable range.

item_total_correlations.note: All items above 0.45 threshold (stricter than 0.25 floor)

inter_item_correlations.note: Highest inter-item correlation is q8–q18 at 0.52, well below 0.70 threshold

cp_knowledge_correlation.note: Down from 0.42 (v1 CP) to 0.36. Significant discriminant validity improvement.

monitoring_recommendations: Track arts_creative q13 item-total correlation (currently 0.46, lowest) — if it drops below 0.40 with real data, refine stem
Monitor CP–Knowledge correlation with real data; target < 0.40, alert if > 0.45
IRT calibration recommended after N ≥ 500 real responses per field
Review option selection frequencies after N ≥ 200 to detect any unpredicted dominance
